topics.name: Pointers & Value vs Reference Semantics
topics.description: Understanding when values are copied vs referenced in Go.

cards.context: In Go, basic types and structs are passed by value (copied). Using a pointer (e.g., *T) passes the address so modifications affect the original. Slices, maps, and channels are reference-like: the header is copied but they refer to shared underlying data.

mcq.question: Which types share underlying data when copied?
mcq.explanation: Slices and maps contain headers that reference underlying data; copying the header results in shared access to that data. Arrays and structs are copied by value.

topics.name: Structs and Interfaces
topics.description: Define data with structs and behavior with interfaces; interfaces are satisfied implicitly.

cards.context: Structs group fields. Methods can be defined on types (value or pointer receivers). Interfaces describe method sets; a type implements an interface by providing those methods.
examples: // A *User implements Greeter because it has Greet()

mcq.question: How does a type satisfy an interface in Go?
mcq.explanation: Go uses implicit interface satisfaction: if a type has the required methods, it implements the interface automatically.

topics.name: Interface Composition
topics.description: Compose interfaces from smaller interfaces to build abstractions.

cards.context: Interfaces can embed other interfaces. This lets you build bigger contracts from small, testable pieces. For example, io.ReadWriteCloser composes Reader, Writer, and Closer.

mcq.question: What is a benefit of interface composition?
mcq.explanation: Composing interfaces encourages small, focused contracts that can be reused and combined without changing implementations.

topics.name: Error Handling Patterns
topics.description: Idiomatic approaches to returning and handling errors in Go.

cards.context: Go favors explicit error returns (value, error). Use sentinel errors, wrapped errors (fmt.Errorf with %w), and errors.Is / errors.As for inspection. Avoid panics for ordinary errors; use them for programmer errors.
examples:
if err != nil { return fmt.Errorf("read failed: %w", err) }
// checking: if errors.Is(err, io.EOF) { ... }

mcq.question: Which function wraps errors for later unwrapping?
mcq.explanation: Use fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err) to wrap an error so it can be inspected with errors.Is and errors.As.

topics.name: Package Organization
topics.description: Best practices for packages, module boundaries, and public APIs.

cards.context: Keep packages small and cohesive. Export only what is necessary. Use clear package names (not plural) and avoid deep nesting. Use internal packages for non-public code and cmd/ for CLI binaries.
examples:
module example.com/project
/ cmd/ - binaries
/ pkg/ - reusable packages
/ internal/ - private packages

mcq.question: Where should non-public helper packages live?
mcq.explanation: Use the internal/ directory to prevent external projects from importing helper packages.

topics.name: defer, panic, recover
topics.description: Control flow for cleanup and handling unexpected panics.

cards.context: Use defer for cleanup (files, mutex unlocks). Panic stops normal execution; recover can catch a panic when called in a deferred function, enabling graceful shutdown or logging.

mcq.question: Where must recover be called to catch a panic?
mcq.explanation: recover only stops a panic when called from a deferred function on the same goroutine where the panic occurred.

topics.name: Generics (Go 1.18+)
topics.description: Type parameters and writing generic functions and data structures.

cards.context: Go 1.18 added generics with type parameters. Use syntax like func Map[T any](s []T, f func(T) T) []T. Constraints allow methods or comparable types.
examples: func Map[T any](s []T, f func(T) T) []T { out := make([]T, len(s)); for i,v := range s { out[i] = f(v) }; return out }

mcq.question: Which keyword denotes an unconstrained type parameter?
mcq.explanation: The predeclared identifier 'any' is an alias for interface{} and denotes an unconstrained type parameter.

topics.name: Goroutines
topics.description: Lightweight concurrency primitives managed by the runtime.

cards.context: Goroutines are cheap to start and multiplexed by the runtime. They communicate via channels or shared memory with synchronization. Monitor goroutine leaks and ensure goroutines exit when work is done.
examples:
go func() { doWork() }()
// ensure goroutine exits by signaling via channel or context

mcq.question: What is a common cause of goroutine leaks?
options: Blocked channel operations or never-exiting loops
mcq.explanation: Goroutine leaks occur when goroutines block forever (waiting on channel operations) or have loops that never exit; use contexts and timeouts to avoid leaks.

topics.name: Channels (Buffered vs Unbuffered)
topics.description: How buffered and unbuffered channels behave and when to use them.

cards.context: Unbuffered channels synchronize sender and receiver: send blocks until receiver is ready. Buffered channels allow limited queuing; send only blocks when buffer is full.
examples:
c := make(chan int)       // unbuffered
c2 := make(chan int, 5)    // buffered with capacity 5

mcq.question: When does a send on a buffered channel block?
mcq.explanation: Sends to a buffered channel block only when its buffer is full.

topics.name: Select Statement
topics.description: Waiting on multiple channel operations with select.

cards.context: Select lets a goroutine wait on multiple channel operations. Use default to avoid blocking, and time.After to implement timeouts.
examples: select { case msg := <-c: fmt.Println(msg) case <-time.After(time.Second): fmt.Println("timeout") }

mcq.question: What can you use to implement a timeout in select?
mcq.explanation: time.After returns a channel that sends the current time after the duration, useful for timeouts in select.

topics.name: Worker Pools
topics.description: Pattern to limit concurrency by using a pool of worker goroutines.

cards.context: Worker pools use a fixed number of goroutines consuming tasks from a job channel and sending results to a results channel. This controls resource usage and backpressure.

mcq.question: What's a benefit of using a worker pool?
mcq.explanation: Worker pools limit concurrency, helping control CPU/memory usage and apply backpressure to producers.

topics.name: Context Package
topics.description: Propagate cancellation, timeouts, and request-scoped values across API boundaries.

cards.context: Use context.Context for cancellation and deadlines. Pass contexts explicitly (not via globals). Derive child contexts with context.WithCancel, WithTimeout, or WithValue.
examples:
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), time.Second*5)
defer cancel()
req = req.WithContext(ctx)

mcq.question: Which function creates a context that can be cancelled?
mcq.explanation: context.WithCancel returns a derived context and a cancel function you call to cancel it.

topics.name: WaitGroup
topics.description: Synchronize goroutines completion using sync.WaitGroup.

cards.context: Use sync.WaitGroup.Add to set number of goroutines, call Done in each goroutine and Wait in the parent to block until completion. Avoid copying WaitGroup values.
examples:
var wg sync.WaitGroup
wg.Add(1)
go func(){ defer wg.Done(); work() }()
wg.Wait()

mcq.question: What happens if you copy a WaitGroup?
options: It may cause race conditions or incorrect counts
mcq.explanation: Copying a WaitGroup can lead to incorrect behavior; it should be passed by pointer and never copied after use.

topics.name: Mutex vs RWMutex
topics.description: Synchronization primitives for exclusive vs concurrent read access.

cards.context: sync.Mutex provides exclusive locking. sync.RWMutex allows multiple readers or one writer. Use RWMutex when reads far outnumber writes for better throughput.
examples:
var mu sync.Mutex
mu.Lock(); // modify shared state; mu.Unlock()
var rw sync.RWMutex
rw.RLock(); // read; rw.RUnlock()

mcq.question: When is RWMutex preferable over Mutex?
mcq.explanation: RWMutex improves performance when many goroutines read shared state and writes are rare.

topics.name: Race Conditions
topics.description: Data races occur when unsynchronized access to shared state happens from multiple goroutines.

cards.context: Use the -race flag to detect race conditions. Prevent races with synchronization (mutexes, channels) or by confining state to a single goroutine.
examples: go run -race ./... // detect races during execution

mcq.question: How do you detect race conditions in Go?
mcq.explanation: The runtime race detector is enabled with the -race flag (go run -race, go test -race).

topics.name: Deadlocks
topics.description: When goroutines wait forever due to circular waits or blocked channels.

cards.context: Deadlocks happen when goroutines wait on each other (e.g., both trying to receive on unbuffered channels with no sender). Avoid by designing clear ownership, using buffered channels, or adding timeouts/context.
examples: // two goroutines waiting on each other without a sender can deadlock; use buffered channels or rearrange logic

mcq.question: What's a common cause of deadlocks in Go?
options: Circular waiting on channels or locks
mcq.explanation: Circular waiting (e.g., goroutine A waits on B while B waits on A) or improper locking leads to deadlocks.

topics.name: REST API Design
topics.description: Designing RESTful endpoints and structuring handlers in Go.

cards.context: Design clear resources, use proper HTTP methods and status codes, and version APIs. In Go, use net/http or frameworks; handlers should be thin and delegate logic to services.
examples:
http.HandleFunc("/v1/users", usersHandler)
// use 201 Created for resource creation, 204 No Content for successful delete

mcq.question: Which status code is appropriate after successful creation?
mcq.explanation: 201 Created indicates a new resource was created.

topics.name: Middleware
topics.description: Reusable HTTP middleware for logging, auth, and recovery.

cards.context: Middleware wraps handlers to provide cross-cutting concerns like logging, authentication, and panic recovery. In Go this is typically implemented as functions that return http.Handler.
examples: func Logging(next http.Handler) http.Handler { return http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request){ log.Println(r.URL.Path); next.ServeHTTP(w,r) }) }

mcq.question: What's a typical use for middleware?
options: Cross-cutting concerns like logging and auth
mcq.explanation: Middleware handles cross-cutting concerns such as logging, authentication, and metrics without changing business logic.

topics.name: Dependency Injection
topics.description: Techniques to inject dependencies for testability and modularity.

cards.context: Use constructor injection (pass interfaces into structs/functions) to make code testable. Avoid service locators and globals. Manual injection is common in Go; DI frameworks are rare.
examples:
type Service struct { Repo Repo }
func NewService(r Repo) *Service { return &Service{Repo: r} }

mcq.question: What's a common DI pattern in Go?
mcq.explanation: Constructor injection passing interfaces makes components testable and decoupled.

topics.name: Logging
topics.description: Structured logging and levels for observability.

cards.context: Use structured logging (key/value) and levels (info, warn, error). Popular libraries: log, logrus, zap. Avoid fmt.Println in servers.
examples: logger.Infof("user created id=%d", id) // or zap.L().Info("user created", zap.Int("id", id))

mcq.question: Why prefer structured logging?
mcq.explanation: Structured logs (key/value) are easier to parse, filter, and query in log systems.

topics.name: Configuration Management
topics.description: Managing config via env, files, and flags.

cards.context: Load configuration from environment variables, files, and command-line flags. Keep secrets out of source control and support per-environment overrides.
examples:
port := os.Getenv("PORT")
flag.String("config", "./config.yml", "path to config")

mcq.question: Where should secrets NOT be stored?
mcq.explanation: Avoid storing secrets in source control; use secret managers or environment variables with proper safeguards.

topics.name: Graceful Shutdown
topics.description: Shutdown servers cleanly on signals, finishing inflight requests.

cards.context: Listen for OS signals (SIGINT/SIGTERM), cancel root context, stop accepting new requests, and wait for inflight requests to finish (server.Shutdown(ctx)).

mcq.question: Which method gracefully shuts down net/http server?
mcq.explanation: Use Server.Shutdown(ctx) to gracefully shutdown, allowing inflight requests to complete within the provided context.

topics.name: Authentication (JWT)
topics.description: Using JWTs for stateless authentication and common considerations.

cards.context: JWTs encode claims and are signed (HMAC or RSA). Validate signature, expiry (exp), and consider revocation strategies. Keep secrets safe and prefer short-lived tokens with refresh tokens.
examples: token, _ := jwt.NewWithClaims(jwt.SigningMethodHS256, claims).SignedString([]byte(secret))

mcq.question: What must you always validate when accepting a JWT?
mcq.explanation: Always validate the token signature and claims like expiry to ensure the token is authentic and still valid.

topics.name: Pagination
topics.description: Best practices for paginating API results.

cards.context: Use cursor-based pagination for large datasets to avoid offset performance issues. Provide metadata (next cursor, limit). Limit max page size to prevent heavy queries.
examples: GET /items?limit=20&cursor=abc123 // return items and next_cursor

mcq.question: Which pagination method scales better for large datasets?
mcq.explanation: Cursor-based pagination avoids expensive OFFSET queries and is more efficient for large, changing datasets.

topics.name: Validation
topics.description: Validating input and request payloads.

cards.context: Validate user input at the boundary. Use libraries (e.g., go-playground/validator) or manual checks. Return clear error messages and avoid trusting client input.
examples:
type User struct { Email string `validate:"required,email"` }
// use validator.New().Struct(user)

mcq.question: Where should validation occur?
mcq.explanation: Server-side validation at the boundary prevents invalid or malicious data from entering the system.
